programs.name: AHCCCS (Arizona Long Term Care System - ALTCS)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Arizona Medicaid covers home care, assisted living, or nursing home costs.

localEntities.name: ALTCS Map available via official site

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with basic activities like bathing, dressing, or taking medications, ALTCS covers the cost of long-term care services through Arizona Medicaid. This includes home care to help your parent stay in their own home, assisted living, memory care, or skilled nursing facility care.

ALTCS also allows you to be paid as your parent's caregiver through Medicaid — income from providing this care doesn't count against eligibility limits. Because ALTCS covers comprehensive long-term care under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers or worry about Medicare's limited coverage for ongoing care needs.

To qualify, your parent must pass a clinical assessment showing they need the level of care typically provided in a nursing home, even if they want to receive care at home. Asset limits are strict: $2,000 for a single person, though the family home and one vehicle don't count if your parent intends to return home.

summary: Needs daily help with basic activities

exemptAssets: Primary home (equity up to $752,000 if intent to return)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or have cognitive deficits from conditions like dementia)
otherRequirements: Arizona resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Social Security Number

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Health-e-Arizona Plus or call (888) 621-6880 — processing time varies as eligibility is determined month-by-month.

steps.title: Complete DE-828 application form
steps.description: Fill out the Arizona Long Term Care System application with your parent's financial and medical information.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, citizenship, Arizona residency, and Social Security Number.

steps.title: Schedule Pre-Admission Screening (PAS)
steps.description: A clinical assessment will determine if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus ALTCS portal, call (888) 621-6880, or submit form DE-828.

applicationGuide.tip: Income from providing care to your parent through ALTCS doesn't count against income limits — this 'Difficulty of Care' exclusion helps family caregivers qualify.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if equity ≤ $752,000 and intent to return or certain family live there)

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security card or proof of application

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily assistance
Recent hospital or nursing home records

contentSections.text: If your parent is married, both spouses' assets count toward the limit even if only one applies. However, the healthy spouse can keep between $32,532 and $162,660 depending on their situation — this is determined case-by-case.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your mom intends to return home or certain family members live there. The home is exempt as long as the equity is $752,000 or less. If equity exceeds this amount, she may still qualify but will need to address the excess equity.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify through an Income Only Trust (also called a Miller Trust). This legal tool allows excess income to be placed in trust, bringing his countable income below the limit. You'll need an elder law attorney to set this up properly.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALTCS allows family members to be paid as caregivers through Medicaid. Even better, this 'Difficulty of Care' income doesn't count against your parent's income limits for eligibility, making it easier for families to qualify while keeping care in the home.

faqs.question: How does the clinical assessment work?
faqs.answer: A Pre-Admission Screening (PAS) evaluates whether your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or mobility. They also assess cognitive deficits from conditions like dementia. Having a diagnosis alone isn't enough — your parent must demonstrate actual functional limitations.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad gave away money in the past few years?
faqs.answer: ALTCS looks back at financial transfers and may impose penalty periods where your dad can't receive benefits. Penalty amounts vary by county: $8,666.72/month in Maricopa, Pima, and Pinal counties, $8,132.22/month in other Arizona counties. Each transfer is evaluated individually.

faqs.question: Does ALTCS cover memory care and assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALTCS covers assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing facilities, as well as in-home services. The goal is to provide care in the least restrictive setting that meets your parent's needs — many people use ALTCS to stay in their own homes longer.

programs.name: ALTCS Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver
programs.tagline: Get paid home care and support services for your parent who needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management.

localEntities.name: Approved plan providers (e.g., via altcs.org list; contact AHCCCS for local)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or has dementia requiring supervision, Arizona's ALTCS program can provide professional home care services to keep them out of a nursing facility. The program covers personal care attendants, transportation assistance, and other support services — and family members (including spouses) can be paid as hired caregivers through approved providers.

Your parent must meet strict financial limits ($2,982/month income, $2,000 in assets) and pass a clinical assessment proving they need Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily help with basic activities or significant cognitive impairment). Because this program combines medical and financial eligibility, failing either requirement disqualifies your parent entirely.

Services are delivered through managed care providers across Arizona. Personal care can include up to 40 hours per week if family provides care (50 hours if non-family), covering assistance with cooking, grooming, toileting, transferring, and basic life skills like managing money or medications.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if living there or intending to return, equity ≤ $752,000)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care through Pre-Admission Screening — clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, toileting, or has cognitive deficits requiring supervision
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or qualified legal resident
Arizona resident intending to remain
Must live in approved setting (own home, licensed facility, or waiver-approved residence)
Must pursue other entitled government benefits

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at azahcccs.gov or call 1-855-432-7587 — processing involves financial review followed by medical assessment interview.

steps.title: Submit Initial Application
steps.description: Complete form DE-828 online at azahcccs.gov, call 1-855-432-7587, or submit by mail to local ALTCS office

steps.title: Financial Evaluation
steps.description: AHCCCS reviews your parent's income, assets, and supporting documents to determine financial eligibility

steps.title: Medical Screening
steps.description: Assigned caseworker conducts Pre-Admission Screening interview to assess Activities of Daily Living and cognitive function

steps.title: Plan Assignment
steps.description: If approved, your parent is assigned to a managed care provider who coordinates all services

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — involves multi-step financial and medical review process
applicationGuide.tip: Gather medical records documenting your parent's need for daily assistance before applying — dementia diagnosis alone isn't sufficient without proof of functional limitations.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $2,000 Limit
excluded: Primary home (if living there or intending to return, equity ≤ $752,000)
Primary home (if spouse, child under 21, or blind/disabled child lives there)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of citizenship or legal residency

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of other government benefits pursued

categories.name: Medical
items: Complete medical history and current physician information
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Documentation of cognitive impairments or dementia diagnosis

contentSections.text: The 60-month Look-Back Rule applies — transfers of assets below fair market value in the past 5 years will cause a penalty period that delays eligibility.
Family caregivers must be hired through the approved managed care provider, not paid directly by your parent. The provider handles all employment paperwork and payments.

faqs.question: Can my mom keep her house if she qualifies for ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit if she lives there or intends to return, as long as home equity doesn't exceed $752,000 (2026 limit). The home is also exempt if you or another family member lives there.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the $2,982 monthly limit?
faqs.answer: She won't qualify — the income limit is strict. However, if she's providing paid care to another ALTCS member in the home, that 'Difficulty of Care' income may be excluded from the calculation.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members including spouses can be paid caregivers, but you must be hired by the approved managed care provider, not paid directly. The provider handles employment paperwork, taxes, and payments up to 40 hours per week for family caregivers.

faqs.question: My dad has dementia — does that automatically qualify him?
faqs.answer: No, dementia diagnosis alone isn't sufficient. He must pass the Pre-Admission Screening proving he needs Nursing Facility Level of Care — requiring daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or significant cognitive supervision needs.

faqs.question: What happens if they have $2,100 in the bank one month?
faqs.answer: They'll lose eligibility. Assets cannot exceed $2,000 in any month to remain qualified. Plan spending down excess assets before they accumulate, or consider exempt purchases like home improvements.

faqs.question: Are there waiting lists for ALTCS services?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify waiting lists, but expect potential delays due to the multi-step financial and medical assessment process required before approval and provider assignment.

programs.name: Arizona PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, medications, and daily support through one coordinated team.

localEntities.name: Specific AZ PACE centers not listed; e.g., InnovAge operates in some states but AZ sites unclear. Use local finder.

programs.intro:
If your parent has been told they need nursing home-level care but you want to keep them at home, Arizona PACE might be the solution. Because PACE covers all medical care under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one team manages everything from doctor visits to medications to daily care.

PACE serves adults 55+ who need significant daily help but can safely live at home with support. The program includes all medical services, prescription drugs, personal care, transportation to appointments, meals, and therapies. For parents with both Medicare and Medicaid, PACE is completely free with no copays or deductibles.

The catch: PACE is only available in specific service areas in Arizona, not statewide. Your parent must live within a PACE organization's coverage area and commit to receiving all their care through the PACE team.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing home level of care (requires daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in a PACE organization's service area
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plans, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE organization for assessment — processing time varies by location.

steps.title: Find your local PACE center
steps.description: Use the National PACE Association finder at npaonline.org to locate PACE organizations in your area, or call AHCCCS at 1-877-382-2363 for referral.

steps.title: Schedule initial assessment
steps.description: PACE staff will evaluate your parent's medical needs and living situation to determine if they meet nursing home level of care requirements.

steps.title: Complete enrollment
steps.description: If eligible, your parent will be assigned to an interdisciplinary care team and begin receiving services at the adult day health center.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies by local PACE center
applicationGuide.waitlist: Wait times may vary by PACE provider location
applicationGuide.tip: Before applying, make sure your parent is willing to switch all their medical care to the PACE team — they cannot keep outside doctors.

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide in Arizona. Your parent must live within a PACE organization's specific service area. Most locations have limited capacity and may have waiting lists.
If your parent has Medicare Advantage, they must disenroll before joining PACE. The PACE team will help coordinate this transition, but plan for a gap in coverage during the switch.

categories.name: Medical Information
items: Recent medical records and test results

categories.name: Personal Information
items: Healthcare proxy or power of attorney documents

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they can't afford $7,000+ per month for private PACE?
faqs.answer: Your parent can still enroll in PACE and pay privately, but costs typically exceed $7,000 monthly. Some families combine private pay with long-term care insurance. Contact local PACE centers for exact pricing and payment options.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires your parent to receive all medical care through the PACE interdisciplinary team. They cannot see outside specialists or use non-PACE providers, except in emergencies.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs to move to a different area of Arizona?
faqs.answer: If they move outside their PACE organization's service area, they must leave the program. PACE coverage areas are limited and don't cover the entire state.

faqs.question: How often does my parent need to go to the PACE day center?
faqs.answer: Frequency depends on your parent's care plan, typically 2-5 days per week. The interdisciplinary team determines the schedule based on medical needs, social benefits, and therapy requirements.

faqs.question: What if my parent doesn't like the PACE program after enrolling?
faqs.answer: PACE enrollment is voluntary and your parent can disenroll at any time. However, they'll need to find new healthcare providers and may face gaps in coverage during the transition back to traditional Medicare.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover home modifications like ramps or bathroom safety equipment?
faqs.answer: Yes, PACE covers home modifications and durable medical equipment as part of comprehensive care. The team assesses your parent's home and provides necessary safety modifications and equipment.

programs.name: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Help paying Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs if your parent's income is under $1,796/month

localEntities.name: AHCCCS central: 701 E. Jefferson St, Phoenix, AZ

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare with monthly income under $1,796 (single) or $2,426 (couple), Arizona's Medicare Savings Programs can pay their Medicare premiums and reduce out-of-pocket costs. The three tiers — QMB, SLMB, and QI — provide different levels of help based on income, with QMB covering the most costs including deductibles and copays.

Because Arizona eliminated asset limits for these programs, your parent can qualify regardless of savings or home value — only monthly income matters. All three programs also provide automatic Extra Help with prescription drug costs, capping generic medications at $12.65 in 2026.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium savings ($185/month) plus potential deductible and copay coverage

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Must apply for other available benefits first

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through AHCCCS at healthearizonaplus.gov or call 855-432-7587 — processing takes 45-90 days.

steps.title: Gather income documentation
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, pension records, and any other monthly income proof for the past month

steps.title: Complete AHCCCS application
steps.description: Fill out the Health Insurance Application online or download form AHCCCS-8011 — include the Medicare Savings Program section

steps.title: Submit with required documents
steps.description: Upload or mail proof of income, Medicare card, ID, and Arizona residency proof

steps.title: Follow up if applying for QI
steps.description: QI programs are first-come-first-served with federal funding caps — apply early in the calendar year for best chances

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI has first-come-first-served with priority for prior recipients; potential waitlist if funds exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year for QI programs since they have federal funding caps and fill up

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part A premium (if applicable), Part B premium (~$185/month), deductibles, coinsurance, and copays. Providers cannot bill you for covered services. Automatic Extra Help for Part D.

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premium only (~$185/month). Automatic Extra Help for Part D prescription drug costs.

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premium only (~$185/month). Automatic Extra Help for Part D. Requires annual reapplication and is first-come-first-served.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2026)
contentSections.footnote: Income calculated after $20 general income disregard. Higher limits for larger households.

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of other benefits applied for

contentSections.text: QI programs have federal funding caps and operate first-come-first-served. Apply early in the calendar year for the best chance of approval.
Arizona has no asset limits for these programs — your parent can qualify regardless of savings, home value, or other resources.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Arizona eliminated asset limits for Medicare Savings Programs, so your parent can own a home, have savings, or other assets and still qualify based solely on monthly income.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: She can still qualify for SLMB, which pays the Medicare Part B premium (about $185/month) and provides automatic Extra Help with prescription drug costs. The main difference is SLMB doesn't cover deductibles and copays like QMB does.

faqs.question: Will providers try to bill my parent even with QMB coverage?
faqs.answer: With QMB, providers are legally prohibited from billing your parent for Medicare-covered services. If this happens, contact AHCCCS immediately. Make sure providers know your parent has 'QMB' status, not just regular Medicaid.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to reapply every year?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB typically have annual renewals managed by AHCCCS. QI requires a fresh application each year and is first-come-first-served, so apply early in January for the best chance.

faqs.question: What counts as income for the limits?
faqs.answer: Social Security, pensions, wages, and most other monthly income count. Arizona applies a $20 general income disregard, so they subtract $20 from total monthly income when determining eligibility.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both this program and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: These are not full Medicaid programs — they only help with Medicare costs. Your parent may qualify for both if their income is low enough, but these programs specifically address Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays.

programs.name: Nutrition Assistance (NA) / SNAP in Arizona
programs.tagline: If your parent is on a fixed income in Arizona, they may qualify for monthly grocery benefits — even with higher income if they're 60+.
programs.intro: If your parent struggles to afford groceries, Arizona's SNAP program (called Nutrition Assistance or NA) provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card for food purchases. Arizona has expanded eligibility beyond federal requirements, making it easier for seniors to qualify. Parents age 60+ get a major advantage: they can bypass the gross income test entirely and qualify based only on net income (after deductions for medical expenses, housing costs, and taxes) and assets. This means your parent could earn $3,000/month but still qualify if their net income after deductions is under $1,304. Minimum benefit is $24/month, with higher amounts based on household size and net income.

otherRequirements: Must have Social Security number
Work requirement: 80 hours/month (many exceptions apply)
Cannot intentionally quit job to qualify

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at arizonaselfhelp.org or call 1-855-777-8590 — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Screen for eligibility
steps.description: Use arizonaselfhelp.org to check if your parent qualifies before starting the full application.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, housing costs, medical expenses, Social Security numbers, and Arizona residency.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete application online, by phone (1-855-777-8590), or at local DES office.

steps.title: While waiting for approval
steps.description: Contact Arizona Food Bank Network for emergency food assistance during processing.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 60+, emphasize their age in the application — they qualify under more favorable income rules.

contentSections.heading: Net Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits (100% Federal Poverty Level). Net income calculated after deductions for taxes, housing, medical expenses.

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Social Security numbers for all household members
Birth dates for all household members
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or proof of income from all sources
Child care or dependent care cost documentation

contentSections.text: Major advantage for seniors: If your parent is 60+, they can qualify even with higher gross income by passing only the net income test. Medical expenses and housing costs are deducted from gross income, so a parent with high medical bills could qualify even with income that seems too high.

Work requirement applies to able-bodied adults under 60, but has many exceptions including disability, pregnancy, caring for disabled family member, or living in high unemployment area. Don't assume your parent is disqualified — check the exceptions.

faqs.question: My mom is 65 and earns $2,500/month from Social Security and a small pension. Can she still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, potentially. Because she's over 60, she only needs to pass the net income test ($1,304/month for one person). If her medical expenses, housing costs, and taxes reduce her net income below $1,304, she qualifies. High medical expenses can make a significant difference.

faqs.question: What foods can't she buy with SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: SNAP cannot be used for alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, hot prepared foods, or non-food items. She can buy groceries, fresh produce, meat, dairy, bread, cereals, and seeds/plants for growing food.

faqs.question: How much will she get each month?
faqs.answer: Benefits vary based on household size and net income. Minimum benefit is $24/month for 1-2 person households. The exact amount depends on her net income after deductions — the lower her net income, the higher her benefit.

faqs.question: Does her house count against the asset limit?
faqs.answer: If she lives in the house, it doesn't count toward the $4,500 asset limit. Her primary vehicle also typically doesn't count. The state looks at bank accounts, investments, and additional properties.

faqs.question: She's not a U.S. citizen but has a green card. Can she apply?
faqs.answer: Yes, green card holders are qualified immigrants for SNAP. Other qualified statuses include refugees, trafficking victims, and certain other immigrant categories. The program isn't limited to U.S. citizens.

faqs.question: What if she needs food help while waiting for approval?
faqs.answer: Contact the Arizona Food Bank Network for emergency food assistance while her application is being processed. Don't wait to apply for SNAP — benefits can be backdated to the application date if approved.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get up to $640/year to help your parent pay heating and cooling bills, plus emergency assistance for utility shutoffs.

localEntities.name: Local Community Action Programs; statewide via ADES

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating or cooling bills, LIHEAP can provide up to $640 per year toward energy costs. This federal program helps Arizona residents with household incomes below $2,807/month for a single person ($3,671 for couples). Because funding is limited and distributed seasonally, families with seniors 60+, disabled members, or young children get priority.

LIHEAP covers two types of assistance: standard benefits ($160-$640 annually for regular heating/cooling costs) and crisis benefits (up to $500 for energy emergencies like shutoff notices). The program runs on seasonal schedules — heating assistance from December through March, cooling assistance from April through October or November depending on your county.

Unlike many programs, LIHEAP has no asset limits, so your parent's savings or home value won't disqualify them. However, they cannot receive benefits if they already have more than $500 in utility bill credits.
programs.savingsSource: Standard benefit $160-$640 plus crisis benefit up to $500

summary: Priority for seniors 60+, disabled, or families with young children
Must be U.S. citizen or qualifying immigrant
otherRequirements: U.S. citizenship, permanent legal residence, or qualifying immigration status (refugee, TPS)
Cannot have existing utility bill credits exceeding $500

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at des.az.gov/liheap or call (866) 494-1981 — processing time varies but apply early as funding runs out quickly.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect ID, proof of citizenship, income verification for last 30 days, current utility bills, and household member information (SSN, birth certificates).

steps.title: Submit application during seasonal window
steps.description: Apply for heating assistance December 1-March 31, or cooling assistance April 1-October 31 (November 30 in some counties). Apply early as funding is limited.

steps.title: Wait for priority processing
steps.description: Applications are processed by priority (seniors 60+, disabled, veterans, families with children under 6 get preference), not first-come-first-served.

steps.title: Receive benefit or crisis assistance
steps.description: Standard benefits go directly to utility companies. Crisis assistance can provide emergency help for shutoff notices or energy emergencies.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — apply early as funding is limited
applicationGuide.waitlist: Agencies may stop accepting applications when funds are exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 60+ or disabled, make sure to highlight this on the application for priority processing.

tiers.name: Standard Benefit
tiers.coverage: $160-$640 per year paid directly to utility company

tiers.name: Crisis Benefit
tiers.coverage: Up to $500 for utility shutoffs or energy emergencies

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status
Proof of residence (lease, deed, or property tax bill)
SSN and birth certificates for all household members

categories.name: Financial
items: Income verification for all adults 18+ for last 30 days (paystubs, Social Security awards, pension statements)
Utility termination or delinquent notice (if applying for crisis assistance)

contentSections.text: LIHEAP funding runs out quickly each season. Apply as soon as the seasonal window opens (December 1 for heating, April 1 for cooling) to avoid missing out.
Seniors 60+, disabled individuals, and veterans get priority processing. If your parent fits these categories, highlight it clearly on the application.

faqs.question: What if my parent already has a credit on their utility bill?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot receive LIHEAP benefits if they already have more than $500 in utility bill credits. Check their current account balance before applying.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for both heating and cooling assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they're separate seasonal applications. Apply for heating assistance December 1-March 31, then apply again for cooling assistance April 1-October 31 (or November 30 depending on county).

faqs.question: What counts as a household for income limits?
faqs.answer: Everyone living together and sharing utility bills counts as one household, including roommates. Use the total household size and combined income to determine eligibility.

faqs.question: How quickly will my parent get help if their utilities are about to be shut off?
faqs.answer: Crisis assistance can provide up to $500 for energy emergencies including shutoff notices, but processing times aren't specified. Apply immediately and mention the emergency situation.

faqs.question: What if my parent applied to tribal LIHEAP first?
faqs.answer: If your parent was denied by tribal LIHEAP, they can apply to the state program. You'll need to provide documentation of the tribal denial.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house or savings disqualify them?
faqs.answer: No, LIHEAP has no asset limits. Your parent's home value, savings accounts, or other assets won't affect their eligibility — only monthly income matters.

programs.name: Arizona Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements could save your parent hundreds on utility bills if their income is under $31,120.

localEntities.name: Pima County Community and Workforce Development (outside Tucson, Sahuarita, Oro Valley, Marana)

programs.intro:
If your parent's income is below $31,120 (single person) and they own or rent their home, they may qualify for free energy efficiency improvements worth up to $9,000. The Arizona Weatherization Assistance Program provides no-cost home retrofits including insulation, air conditioning repair, duct sealing, and energy-efficient appliances to help reduce monthly utility bills.

Because the program operates through different agencies depending on your parent's utility company and location, you'll need to contact the right administrator for their area. Priority goes to elderly residents, disabled individuals, and families with children, making this particularly relevant for aging parents.

otherRequirements: Must be homeowner or renter
Must be customer of participating utility (SRP, TEP, UniSource, or other Arizona providers)
Home must pass assessment and inspection

applicationGuide.summary: Call the administrator for your utility service area — processing time varies by region and funding availability.

steps.title: Identify your utility provider
steps.description: Check your electric bill to see if you have SRP, TEP, UniSource, or another Arizona utility company.

steps.title: Contact the right administrator
steps.description: Call the phone number for your specific utility service area and county (see directory below).

steps.title: Complete intake screening
steps.description: Provide income information and basic household details to determine initial eligibility.

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A technician will inspect the home to identify which energy efficiency improvements are needed and feasible.

steps.title: Await approval and scheduling
steps.description: If approved, contractors will schedule the free installation of approved improvements like insulation, duct sealing, or appliance replacement.

applicationGuide.tip: Call early in the year when funding is typically renewed — programs often have waiting lists by mid-year.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (200% Federal Poverty Level)
contentSections.footnote: Add $10,760 yearly for each additional person. Limits vary slightly by utility provider.

offices.name: Tucson and South Tucson (TEP service area)
Pima County outside Tucson/Sahuarita/Oro Valley/Marana
offices.organization: Pima County Community and Workforce Development

offices.name: SRP Service Area (Maricopa County outside Phoenix/Mesa)
City of Phoenix
offices.address: 200 W. Washington Street 4th Floor, Phoenix, AZ 85003

contentSections.text: This program has no single statewide application. You must contact the specific administrator for your parent's utility company and county — using the wrong office will delay the process.
Income limits are 200% of federal poverty guidelines, which is higher than many programs. A single person can earn up to $31,120 and still qualify.

faqs.question: What if my parent rents — can they still get these improvements?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters can qualify, but the landlord may need to provide written permission for major improvements like insulation or HVAC work. The program administrator will explain what's needed during the home assessment.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to pay anything upfront or get reimbursed later?
faqs.answer: No. All approved improvements are completely free with no upfront costs, deposits, or reimbursement process. Qualified contractors are paid directly by the program.

faqs.question: What happens if their income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary slightly by utility provider — SRP adds $10,760 per additional household member while TEP/UniSource add $11,000. If they're borderline, it's worth calling to confirm the exact threshold for their area.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on their electric bill?
faqs.answer: Savings depend on which improvements are made and the home's current condition. The program can provide up to $9,000 in improvements, but specific bill reductions aren't guaranteed and vary by home.

faqs.question: Can they get improvements if they already upgraded their home recently?
faqs.answer: Maybe. The required home assessment determines which improvements are still needed and cost-effective. Previous upgrades won't disqualify them, but may limit which additional work is approved.

faqs.question: Is there a waiting list, and how long does the whole process take?
faqs.answer: Funding is limited and processing times aren't published. Contact your local administrator early in the year when funding is typically renewed, as programs often develop waiting lists by mid-year.

programs.name: Arizona State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free Medicare counseling to help your parent navigate coverage options and save money on health insurance.

localEntities.name: Statewide helpline and azship.org; local examples: PCOA Tucson (520-790-7262), SEAGO Bisbee; full network via 1-800-432-4040

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare or is approaching Medicare age, Arizona SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help them understand their options and avoid costly mistakes. Because Medicare has dozens of plan choices with different costs and coverage, SHIP counselors help compare plans, apply for money-saving programs, and handle appeals or problems.

SHIP counselors can help your parent apply for Medicare Savings Programs (which can save up to $8,400/year on Medicare premiums and costs) and Extra Help with prescription drug costs. They also provide fraud prevention through the Senior Medicare Patrol program. The service is available to all Medicare beneficiaries and their families — no income limits apply, though priority goes to those with limited incomes or disabilities.

summary: Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver
otherRequirements: Must be seeking help with Medicare-related issues

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-800-432-4040 and counselors will return your call to schedule an appointment.

steps.title: Call the statewide helpline
steps.description: Call 1-800-432-4040 or fill out the intake form at azship.org

steps.title: Schedule counseling
steps.description: A local counselor will return your call and schedule a phone, virtual, or in-person appointment

steps.title: Prepare information
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card and any current plan documents ready for the session

applicationGuide.processingTime: Counselors return calls or schedule appointments promptly after contact
applicationGuide.tip: Services rely on volunteer availability, so you may get a call-back rather than immediate assistance.

offices.name: Division of Aging and Adult Services
offices.address: 1789 West Jefferson, Site Code MD6288, Phoenix, AZ 85007

contentSections.text: SHIP provides counseling only — they don't pay for healthcare or provide direct financial aid. But they can help your parent apply for other programs that do provide money-saving benefits.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SHIP and Medicare customer service?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors are independent — they don't work for insurance companies or Medicare. Because they're not trying to sell anything, they can give unbiased advice about which plan might save your parent the most money.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent already has Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP helps with ongoing issues like appeals, billing problems, coverage questions, and comparing plans during open enrollment. They can also check if your parent qualifies for programs to reduce their Medicare costs.

faqs.question: How much does SHIP counseling cost?
faqs.answer: All SHIP services are completely free. They're funded by federal and state grants, not by insurance companies or fees.

faqs.question: Can I get help for my parent if I don't live in Arizona?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as your parent lives in Arizona and has Medicare. SHIP counselors can work with family members who live out of state and help coordinate your parent's care.

faqs.question: What if my parent makes too much money for other assistance programs?
faqs.answer: SHIP has no income limits. Even high-income Medicare beneficiaries can get free counseling to compare plans and understand their options. The counselors prioritize people with limited incomes, but they help everyone.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get an appointment?
faqs.answer: Counselors return calls promptly, but since services depend on volunteer and staff availability, you might experience some delay during busy periods like Medicare open enrollment.

programs.name: Congregate and Home-Delivered Meals
programs.tagline: Free nutritious meals delivered to your parent's home or served at local senior centers if they're 60+ and struggling to prepare food.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and has trouble preparing meals due to illness, disability, or mobility issues, Arizona provides free nutritious meals either delivered to their home or served at senior centers. Home-delivered meals include hot entrées in 3-compartment containers plus cold items like salad, fruit, and dessert — up to 7 meals per week, with special diets available for medical needs. Each delivery also includes a wellness check on your parent.

Congrigate meals are hot weekday lunches served at senior centers, designed for both nutrition and social connection. Your parent can receive either home-delivered or congregate meals, but not both on the same day — maximum one meal per day from the program.

Eligibility prioritizes those with the greatest social and economic need, including low-income seniors, minorities, and rural residents. While there are no fixed statewide income limits, financial screening through the Area Agency on Aging determines whether meals are free or if your parent qualifies for financial assistance.

summary: Homebound or unable to prepare meals
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or unable to prepare meals independently due to illness, disability, or mobility issues
otherRequirements: Adults 18-59 with disabilities may qualify for some programs
Referral often required from Area Agency on Aging or case managers

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Area Agency on Aging helpline for eligibility screening — no application form required.

steps.title: Call for screening
steps.description: Contact Area Agency on Aging at 602-264-4357 for eligibility assessment and referral to local providers

steps.title: Complete intake
steps.description: Provide information about your parent's age, living situation, ability to prepare meals, and financial need during phone screening

steps.title: Get matched to provider
steps.description: AAA will connect you with local meal providers like YWCA or community centers based on your parent's location and needs

steps.title: Start meal delivery
steps.description: Provider will schedule meal delivery or congregate dining based on availability and your parent's preferences

applicationGuide.waitlist: Scheduling varies by provider and demand in your area
applicationGuide.tip: Have information ready about your parent's medical conditions and dietary restrictions — special diets are available for diabetes, heart conditions, and other health needs.

offices.name: Area Agency on Aging Statewide Helpline
offices.address: Primary screening for all meal programs

categories.name: Personal
items: Your parent's age and disability status if under 60
Description of why they can't prepare meals (illness, mobility, etc.)

categories.name: Medical
items: List of dietary restrictions or special diet needs
Current medications that affect eating or nutrition
Any recent hospitalizations or health changes

contentSections.text: You must call the Area Agency on Aging helpline first at 602-264-4357 — don't contact meal providers directly. They require referral screening to determine eligibility and match your parent with the right local provider.
If your parent has Medicaid/ALTCS coverage, contact their health plan or case manager directly — they may cover meals through a different pathway without income screening.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for free meals?
faqs.answer: Private pay options are available for about $9.49 per meal if your parent doesn't qualify for free meals. Some providers also offer self-pay programs for seniors 80+ or those recovering from hospitalization regardless of income.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both home-delivered and congregate meals?
faqs.answer: No, your parent can only receive one meal per day from the program — either home-delivered or congregate dining, but not both on the same day. They can switch between options based on their needs and health.

faqs.question: How many meals per week will my parent receive?
faqs.answer: Home-delivered meals can be up to 7 per week, while congregate meals are typically hot lunches on weekdays. Some areas provide weekday hot meals plus frozen weekend meals. The exact schedule depends on your local provider and availability.

faqs.question: What if my parent has diabetes or other dietary restrictions?
faqs.answer: Special diets are available for medical conditions like diabetes, heart disease, and other health needs. Make sure to discuss your parent's dietary requirements during the initial screening call.

faqs.question: Is there a waiting list for meal delivery?
faqs.answer: Scheduling varies by provider and demand in your area. Popular programs may have wait times, so apply as soon as you notice your parent having difficulty preparing meals independently.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent is temporarily hospitalized?
faqs.answer: Contact your meal provider about pausing delivery during hospitalization. Some programs specifically serve seniors who are recovering post-hospital and may be able to restart meals when your parent returns home.

programs.name: Arizona Long Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your parent has problems with their nursing home, assisted living, or adult day care facility.

localEntities.name: Maricopa County Area Agency on Aging
NACOG (serving Apache, Coconino, Navajo, and Yavapai counties)
Navajo Nation Area Agency on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a nursing home, assisted living facility, adult day care, hospice center, or adult foster care home in Arizona, the Long Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy when problems arise. Because ombudsmen are trained investigators who understand facility regulations, they can resolve complaints about abuse, neglect, inappropriate eviction, poor food quality, or medication errors that families often struggle to address alone.

This is not a benefit program that provides money or services. Instead, ombudsmen investigate complaints, mediate disputes between your parent and facility staff, and provide information about resident rights and facility regulations. The program serves any resident of a covered long-term care facility in Arizona — no income, age, or health requirements apply.

summary: Resident of Arizona long-term care facility
Covers nursing homes, assisted living, adult day care
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in Arizona (nursing homes, hospice centers, adult day care, assisted living facilities, or adult foster care homes)

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging office — no forms required and services begin immediately.

steps.title: Contact your regional office
steps.description: Call the Area Agency on Aging number for your parent's county. You can also call the state office at (602) 542-6454 extension 9.

steps.title: Describe the problem
steps.description: Explain the issue your parent is experiencing at their facility. The ombudsman will ask for basic information about the facility and the complaint.

steps.title: Get resident consent
steps.description: The ombudsman must have your parent's consent before investigating. They will speak directly with your parent to get permission to proceed.

steps.title: Investigation begins
steps.description: The ombudsman visits the facility, reviews records, and works to resolve the complaint through mediation or referral to appropriate agencies.

applicationGuide.tip: Keep detailed notes about incidents, including dates, times, and staff involved — this helps ombudsmen investigate more effectively.

urls.label: Arizona Long Term Care Ombudsman Program

offices.name: State Office (All Counties)
offices.address: 1789 W. Jefferson Street, Mail Drop 6288, Phoenix, AZ 85007

contentSections.text: The ombudsman must get your parent's consent before investigating any complaint — they cannot act on family concerns alone, even if your parent has dementia or other cognitive issues.
If you're not satisfied with your regional ombudsman's response, escalate to the DES oversight office at (602) 542-6446, not the main program number.

faqs.question: Can I file a complaint on behalf of my parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman must speak directly with your parent to get consent before investigating, even if your parent has cognitive issues. If your parent cannot provide meaningful consent, the ombudsman will assess the situation and may still be able to help through information and guidance.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my parent for filing a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal. The ombudsman investigates retaliation complaints and can refer cases to Adult Protective Services at (877) 767-2385 or other regulatory agencies for enforcement action.

faqs.question: How is this different from calling Adult Protective Services?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen focus on facility-related complaints and resident rights, while Adult Protective Services investigates abuse and neglect. For suspected abuse or neglect, you should also report to Adult Protective Services at (877) 767-2385 — both agencies can work on the same case.

faqs.question: Can the ombudsman help if the facility is trying to evict my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Inappropriate eviction is a common complaint ombudsmen investigate. They understand facility discharge regulations and can help determine if the eviction follows proper procedures and requirements.

faqs.question: What types of facilities does this program cover?
faqs.answer: The program covers nursing homes, assisted living facilities, adult foster care homes, hospice centers, and adult day care programs. If you're unsure whether your parent's facility qualifies, call your regional office.

faqs.question: Will the ombudsman visit my parent's facility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ombudsmen conduct on-site visits to investigate complaints, observe conditions, review records, and speak with residents and staff. They have legal authority to access facilities and resident records with consent.

stateOverview.intro: Arizona offers 10 senior programs that could save your parent hundreds or thousands of dollars on medical care, groceries, utilities, and daily support. Eight provide direct financial benefits or services — from AHCCCS covering nursing home costs to SNAP providing monthly grocery money. Two offer free resources like Medicare counseling and advocacy help. Arizona's ALTCS (Arizona Long Term Care System) is the state's version of Medicaid long-term care, covering home care, assisted living, and nursing homes for parents who need daily help with basic activities. Start by checking if your parent qualifies for AHCCCS or Medicare Savings Programs — these create the foundation for accessing other benefits.

startHere.name: AHCCCS (Arizona Long Term Care System)
startHere.why: Covers the biggest expenses — home care, assisted living, or nursing home costs if your parent needs daily help.

startHere.name: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs
startHere.why: Reduces Medicare costs immediately if your parent's income is under $1,796/month.

startHere.name: Nutrition Assistance (SNAP)
startHere.why: Monthly grocery benefits with higher income limits for seniors 60+.

startHere.name: Arizona SHIP
startHere.why: Free Medicare counseling helps you navigate all health insurance options before applying for other programs.

byNeed.need: Help paying for home care or assisted living
byNeed.description: Both cover daily care costs if your parent needs help with bathing, dressing, or medication management.
Medicare Savings Programs reduce premiums and out-of-pocket costs; PACE provides all medical care through one coordinated team.
SNAP provides monthly grocery money; meal programs deliver free prepared food to seniors 60+.
LIHEAP pays heating and cooling bills; weatherization provides free home energy improvements.
SHIP helps with Medicare questions; Ombudsman advocates when care facilities have problems.

quickFacts: Arizona's Medicaid program is called AHCCCS (Arizona Health Care Cost Containment System)
SNAP benefits can be higher for seniors 60+ even if their income exceeds regular limits
LIHEAP provides up to $640/year for utility assistance in Arizona
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Eight Arizona programs provide direct benefits that require income or health qualifications — like AHCCCS covering care costs or SNAP providing grocery money. Two programs offer free resources available to any Arizona senior regardless of income: SHIP provides Medicare counseling and the Ombudsman advocates for residents in care facilities. Start with the free resources to understand your options, then apply for benefits your parent qualifies for.
